title: An Analytical Model for Flow Induced by a Constant-Head Pumping in a Leaky Unconfined Aquifer System with Considering Unsaturated Flow

research.question: How do unsaturated-zone drainage, lower-layer leakage, partial penetration, and skin affect drawdown and wellbore flow during a constant-head test in an unconfined aquifer?
research.system: An infinite radial three-layer system with an unsaturated zone, an unconfined aquifer, and a lower aquifer, pumped at constant head through a partially penetrating screened well with skin.
research.inputData: Hydraulic and storage properties of all layers, Gardner unsaturated parameters, layer thicknesses, well geometry and head, plus FEMWATER simulations and a 24-hour Iowa field constant-head test.
research.method: Couple saturated groundwater equations to Richards' equation, linearize by perturbation with Gardner constitutive curves, solve by Laplace and Weber transforms, derive wellbore flow, examine sensitivity and limiting behavior, validate numerically, and estimate field parameters.
research.benchmark: Tartakovsky-Neuman constant-rate drawdown, Chang et al. homogeneous-aquifer behavior, FEMWATER saturated-unsaturated simulations, and Jones et al. observation-well data.
research.uncertainty: Normalized sensitivity indicates parameter influence and field fits report SEE and ME. The analytical linearization fixes the water table for small drawdown; FEMWATER deviations in the unsaturated zone reach about 0.05 m for one case.
research.conclusion: The coupled analytical solution reproduces numerical and field drawdown, shows strong control by radial conductivity and saturated thickness, and quantifies when unsaturated thickness or lower-layer leakage may be simplified.

narrative.throughline: Holding a well at constant head makes its declining flow rate a visible ledger of water released from the aquifer, unsaturated zone, and lower layer.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows pumping lowers groundwater but may assume all extracted water comes from the saturated aquifer.
narrative.stakes: Ignoring unsaturated drainage or leakage can distort constant-head test interpretation and underestimate hydraulic conductivities.
narrative.resolution: The three water sources reunite at the wellbore flow curve, with explicit thresholds and the small-drawdown linearization visible.

background.premise: A constant-head test fixes well drawdown and measures the time-varying wellbore flow rate.
background.visibleEvidence: The well water level remains pinned while the extraction arrow narrows through time.
background.whyNow: This defines the observable object that differs from a constant-rate pumping test.
background.boundary: The model targets low-permeability unconfined settings where constant head is operationally useful.
background.sourceExcerpt: water ing the test, the well water level is kept constant and
background.visualObject: Pinned well head with a narrowing pumping arrow
background.motionPurpose: Separate the fixed boundary from the changing response.
background.stepDetail: Pin drawdown at the screen, start pumping, and reduce the flow arrow as storage sources evolve.
background.transitionBridge: The changing arrow is traced into the layers that supply it.

background.premise: Unconfined pumping draws sequentially from elastic storage and delayed gravity drainage.
background.visibleEvidence: A drawdown curve passes from early elastic response through a delayed-drainage plateau toward late confined behavior.
background.whyNow: The time-regime geometry motivates explicit unsaturated flow above the water table.
background.boundary: The paper models the unsaturated zone with Gardner's exponential constitutive relation.
background.sourceExcerpt: early pumping period, the pumped water is mainly from the elastics storage
background.visualObject: Three-stage unconfined drawdown curve linked to aquifer and vadose-zone water parcels
background.motionPurpose: Reveal when each water source enters the pumping response.
background.stepDetail: Release elastic storage first, drain unsaturated pores next, and let the curve approach late confined behavior.
background.transitionBridge: The layered sources become the model's three coupled domains.

background.premise: No prior constant-head model combined unsaturated flow with leakage from a second aquifer for this system.
background.visibleEvidence: A three-layer cross-section shows both the upper unsaturated pathway and lower leakage pathway missing from a legacy well model.
background.whyNow: This states the paper-specific modeling gap.
background.boundary: The new model assumes infinite lateral extent and no-flow outer vertical boundaries.
background.sourceExcerpt: well To our knowledge, there is no research on the CHT conducted
background.visualObject: Unsaturated-aquifer-lower-layer stack around a partially penetrating well
background.motionPurpose: Restore both omitted source pathways around the constant-head well.
background.stepDetail: Build the middle aquifer, add the unsaturated layer above, add the second aquifer below, and preserve the partial screen and skin.
background.transitionBridge: Each domain receives its governing balance.

methods.input: A partially penetrating constant-head well in an anisotropic unconfined aquifer
methods.output: A radial-vertical groundwater equation with skin-modified inner boundary
methods.validity: Groundwater flow obeys the saturated flow equation and the thin skin is represented by a dimensionless skin factor.
methods.sourceExcerpt: are key properties of the un- ing governing equation for aquifer drawdown:
methods.visualObject: Radial and vertical flow arrows converging on the screened well through a thin skin
methods.motionPurpose: Connect fixed screen head to anisotropic saturated flow and skin loss.
methods.stepDetail: Balance radial and vertical flux with storage, apply uniform screen flux, collapse the thin skin into Sk, and set zero far-field drawdown.
methods.transitionBridge: The water-table interface connects this saturated field to unsaturated drainage.

methods.input: Pressure head above the water table and Gardner soil characteristic curves
methods.output: Richards' equation with pressure-dependent conductivity and moisture capacity
methods.validity: Gardner's exponential model links pressure head to relative conductivity and specific moisture capacity.
methods.sourceExcerpt: basis of Eqs. (12)–(17), the drawdown distribution described by Richards’ and equation
methods.visualObject: Unsaturated moisture profile feeding the water table
methods.motionPurpose: Turn pore drainage above the water table into a pressure-dependent flow field.
methods.stepDetail: Map pressure head to saturation, map saturation to conductivity, differentiate moisture content, and place the resulting coefficients in Richards' equation.
methods.transitionBridge: A continuity pair joins the unsaturated and saturated fields.

methods.output: First-order equations on a fixed initial water table
methods.validity: Pumping drawdown is assumed small relative to aquifer thickness, allowing second and higher perturbation terms to be neglected.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Note that as ε is small enough, the second or- ∂z = 0, z = b + bu (22) der and higher order terms on the right-hand side of Eq. (32)
methods.visualObject: Moving water table settling onto a fixed reference line as higher-order terms fade
methods.motionPurpose: Make the analytical approximation and its boundary visible.
methods.stepDetail: Expand all fields in epsilon, retain first order, fix the water table at z = b, and evaluate Gardner coefficients at the zero-order pressure profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The linearized layered problem is transformable.

methods.input: The linearized radial layered initial-boundary problem
methods.output: Time-dependent layer equations represented in the Laplace domain
methods.validity: The paper develops the drawdown and wellbore-flowrate solutions by the Laplace transform method.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.3; Supplementary Material Appendix C
methods.sourceExcerpt: wellbore ﬂowrate are developed based on the methods of Laplace
methods.visualObject: Three time-dependent layer equations entering a Laplace-domain plane
methods.motionPurpose: Isolate the temporal transformation from the radial treatment.
methods.stepDetail: Map each time-dependent layer equation into the Laplace domain.
methods.transitionBridge: The Laplace-domain equations retain radial dependence for the Weber projection.

methods.input: Laplace-domain equations with radial dependence outside a finite-radius well
methods.output: Laplace-Weber equations on a radial domain extending from the well radius to infinity
methods.validity: The Weber transform applies to radially symmetric diffusion problems with a finite inner radius.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.3; Supplementary Material Appendix B
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Weber transform can be considered as a Hankel
methods.visualObject: Radial layer profiles projected onto Weber kernel modes
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the finite-radius radial geometry after the time transformation.
methods.stepDetail: Project each radial equation onto the Weber kernel.
methods.transitionBridge: The transformed layer equations can now be solved subject to their interfaces.

methods.input: Laplace-Weber equations for the unsaturated zone unconfined aquifer and second layer
methods.output: Transformed drawdown expressions for the unconfined aquifer unsaturated zone and second layer
methods.validity: Equations 38 through 40 report one transformed drawdown expression for each modeled layer.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.3, Equations (38)-(40); Supplementary Material Appendix C
methods.sourceExcerpt: drawdown in the unconﬁned aquifer, unsaturated zone, and second
methods.visualObject: Three aligned transformed drawdown fields labeled by layer
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the separate analytical result for every coupled domain.
methods.stepDetail: Solve the transformed boundary-value problem for each layer.
methods.transitionBridge: The aquifer solution supplies the well-face drawdown gradient.

methods.input: Aquifer drawdown at the finite-radius well face
methods.output: The radial drawdown gradient governing local screen flux
methods.validity: The inner boundary condition relates radial drawdown gradient to the uniform flux across the screen.
methods.sourceExcerpt: At the edge of the wellbore, the ﬂux across the screen
methods.visualObject: Radial drawdown slope touching the screened well face
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the solved aquifer field into a local well-face flux driver.
methods.stepDetail: Differentiate aquifer drawdown radially at the well face.
methods.transitionBridge: The local gradient extends over the partially penetrating screen.

methods.input: Well-face radial drawdown gradient over the screen from z1 to b
methods.output: A screen-averaged well-face relation including the dimensionless skin factor
methods.validity: Equation 10 integrates the radial gradient over the screened interval and incorporates the skin factor from Equation 9.
methods.sourceExcerpt: then be obtained by substituting Eq. (9) into Eq. (8) and arranged
methods.visualObject: Gradient arrows collected along the partial screen beside a skin ring
methods.motionPurpose: Aggregate local screen flux into the well response while retaining skin loss.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate the radial flux over the screened interval.
methods.transitionBridge: The integrated transformed response is returned to pumping time.

methods.output: Time-domain response amplitudes retaining the Weber coordinate
methods.validity: The numerical inversion uses the Crump algorithm identified by the paper.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.3, inversion paragraph after Equation (41)
methods.sourceExcerpt: using numerical Laplace inverse transform method developed by Crump (1976).
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain layer amplitudes unfolding along pumping time
methods.motionPurpose: Restore temporal behavior before radial inversion.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the Crump inversion to each transformed response amplitude.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-domain amplitudes still require inverse Weber integration over radial modes.

methods.input: Time-domain response amplitudes indexed by the Weber transform variable
methods.output: Physical drawdown fields and dimensionless wellbore flowrate QD
methods.validity: The Weber inverse is evaluated by numerical integration over its transform variable.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.3, inversion paragraph after Equation (41)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Weber inverse transform can be achieved by
methods.visualObject: Weber modes integrated into radial drawdown and QD curves
methods.motionPurpose: Return the temporal modal response to physical radial space.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate the inverse Weber kernel over the transform coordinate.
methods.transitionBridge: The recovered response can be compared with its source limiting solution.

methods.input: Present normalized drawdown and the Tartakovsky-Neuman constant-rate solution
methods.output: Constant-head and constant-rate normalized-drawdown equivalence after the source time criterion
methods.validity: The comparison removes leakage, well radius, well skin, and finite unsaturated thickness; the stated criterion is t at least 5 Ss r squared divided by Kr.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the problems of CHT under the time criterion t ≥ 5Ssr2/Kr.
methods.visualObject: Present and Tartakovsky-Neuman normalized-drawdown curves on one time axis
methods.motionPurpose: Mark the source condition under which the pumping formulations agree.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay both normalized-drawdown curves and mark the time-criterion boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: The source response is next perturbed to identify parameter influence.

methods.output: Time-dependent influence curves for aquifer unsaturated and lower-layer parameters
methods.validity: A 10^-3 finite-difference perturbation approximates the normalized derivative.
methods.sourceExcerpt: that Q is very sensitive to the change in parameters b and
methods.visualObject: Sensitivity curves grouped by saturated unsaturated and lower-layer ownership
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal which physical layer controls each part of the flow record.
methods.stepDetail: Perturb each parameter, normalize Q change, align curve peaks with time, and distinguish dominant saturated-aquifer controls from weaker sources.
methods.transitionBridge: The parameter study supplies the source sweeps used to identify simplification thresholds.

methods.input: Wellbore-flowrate curves for dimensionless unsaturated exponent alphaD from 1 to 1000
methods.output: Linearized water-table simplification at alphaD equal to or greater than 100
methods.validity: The threshold comes from the reported one-day CHT comparison with the homogeneous-aquifer solution and is not universal.
methods.sourceExcerpt: for αD = 100 matches with the solution of Chang et al. (2010a)
methods.visualObject: AlphaD sweep converging on the linearized-water-table QD curve
methods.motionPurpose: Expose when explicit unsaturated-zone behavior reduces to the simpler boundary representation.
methods.stepDetail: Align the alphaD curves and mark their agreement at 100.
methods.transitionBridge: A separate thickness sweep determines when the upper boundary becomes irrelevant.

methods.input: Wellbore-flowrate curves for unsaturated-thickness ratio zeta_u from zero to infinity
methods.output: Infinite-thickness simplification at zeta_u equal to or greater than 0.5
methods.validity: The threshold is conditional on the parameter values used for Figure 6.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the value of ζ u can be considered as inﬁnity as long as ζ u ≥ 0.5
methods.visualObject: Unsaturated-thickness curves merging at the half-thickness marker
methods.motionPurpose: Locate the modeled thickness beyond which the upper boundary no longer changes QD.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the thickness-ratio curves and mark their merger at 0.5.
methods.transitionBridge: The lower-layer conductivity sweep supplies the leakage threshold.

methods.input: Wellbore-flowrate curves for lower-layer conductivity ratio kappa_z
methods.output: Negligible lower-layer leakage below kappa_z of 0.01
methods.validity: The threshold is the Figure 7 model-case result for the stated aquifer and layer parameters.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the ratio of Kz /Kz is less than 0.01.
methods.visualObject: Lower-layer leakage arrows fading below the kappa_z threshold
methods.motionPurpose: Show when the second layer ceases to alter wellbore flowrate materially.
methods.stepDetail: Move kappa_z below 0.01 and fade the lower-layer contribution.
methods.transitionBridge: The threshold-bounded analytical response is checked against the moving-water-table simulation.

methods.input: Analytical drawdown and matched saturated-unsaturated FEMWATER scenarios
methods.output: Close saturated-zone agreement and a bounded unsaturated-zone deviation
methods.validity: FEMWATER permits a moving phreatic surface and therefore tests the fixed-water-table approximation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: predicted by the present solution are close to those of numerical simulations.
methods.visualObject: Analytical and FEMWATER drawdown curves at two radii and two elevations
methods.motionPurpose: Expose where the linearized analytical solution agrees and where its approximation becomes visible.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay curves for two alpha values, compare saturated and unsaturated observation points, and shade the maximum unsaturated discrepancy.
methods.transitionBridge: Validated curves can be fitted to the Iowa constant-head test.

methods.input: Drawdown records from four Iowa observation wells
methods.output: Five-parameter estimates whose predicted curves match the field data
methods.validity: Levenberg-Marquardt fitting is assessed by SEE and ME and compared with a solution omitting unsaturated flow.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Fig. 9 shows the drawdown predicted by our the unsaturated ﬂow diminishes at the late times. When solution (solid line) match well with the ﬁeld data.
methods.visualObject: Four observed drawdown series with coupled and unsaturated-omitting fitted curves
methods.motionPurpose: Return the model to the test wells and show the bias incurred by removing unsaturated flow.
methods.stepDetail: Fit each well record, overlay predictions, compare error measures, and contrast conductivity estimates with the unsaturated-omitting model.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted layer contributions reunite at the constant-head well.

return.visualObject: The constant-head well in the three-layer system with source arrows scaled by their time-dependent contributions and field-fitted drawdown curves
return.claim: Explicitly coupling unsaturated drainage and lower-layer leakage yields an analytical constant-head response that agrees with numerical and field evidence for the tested conditions.
return.claimBoundary: The solution assumes small drawdown with a fixed initial water table, Gardner exponential retention, infinite lateral extent, linearized flow, and prescribed layer geometry; reported simplification thresholds are model-case results rather than universal constants.
return.sourceExcerpt: of dimensionless param- ment model FEMWATER, which can deal with the problems

visual.primaryObject: Partially penetrating constant-head well in an unsaturated-unconfined-lower-aquifer stack
visual.accentObject: Time-varying source arrows from elastic storage gravity drainage and leakage
visual.geometryNotes: Use a radial cross-section with stable layer thicknesses and a fixed analytical water-table reference. Preserve the well screen and skin geometry while source arrows change through time; show FEMWATER's moving phreatic surface only as the comparison object.
